title: Probing Floquet topological phases via non-Hermitian skin effect of reflected waves
abstract: Periodically driven systems host topological phases without static analogs, such as the anomalous Floquet phase characterized by trivial bulk bands yet robust boundary modes. In this work, we investigate the scattering problem of a Floquet Chern insulator and reveal the non-Hermitian skin effect (NHSE) of reflected waves. Using a discrete-time scattering formalism, we demonstrate how the non-Hermitian winding number of the reflection matrix is linked to the bulk Floquet invariant via boundary resonances. This reflected-wave NHSE relies on which quasienergy gap the incident wave resides in, leading to a gap-dependent Goos-Hänchen (GH) shift. We further show that the momentum-integrated GH shift quantitatively yields the Floquet topological invariant of the corresponding gap. Our work highlights a frequency-dependent NHSE of reflected waves in driven systems and provides a real-space scattering approach to identify non-equilibrium topology.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the topological properties of Floquet insulators can be probed via the scattering of waves. It shows that the non-Hermitian skin effect in reflected waves and the resulting Goos-Hänchen shift directly reveal the bulk Floquet invariants. This offers a practical real-space method to identify complex topological phases like the anomalous Floquet phase.
main_problem: How to diagnose the bulk topological properties of periodically driven (Floquet) systems, particularly the anomalous Floquet phase, using a real-space scattering approach.
main_result: The authors establish that the non-Hermitian skin effect (NHSE) of reflected waves and the momentum-integrated Goos-Hänchen (GH) shift can quantitatively recover the bulk Floquet topological invariant.
method: A discrete-time scattering formalism is used to derive the reflection matrix and analyze its spectral properties and phase winding.
model_or_system: A 2D Floquet Chern insulator modeled as a multi-step driving lattice on a bipartite square lattice, coupled to two semi-infinite, topologically trivial leads.
key_observables: Non-Hermitian winding number of the reflection matrix, Goos-Hänchen (GH) shift, and the Floquet topological invariant (W3).
important_parameters: Driving period (tau), driving strength (J), quasienergy gaps (0 and pi), and the wide-band limit for leads.
main_assumptions: The system operates in the wide-band limit (energy-independent density of states in leads) and assumes a sufficiently long system to suppress bulk transmission.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the lattice model and scattering setup; Fig 2 compares the reflection matrix eigenspectra under PBC and OBC; Fig 3 shows the spatial localization of reflection eigenvectors; Fig 4 displays the momentum-dependent GH shift across different topological phases.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scattering setup, develops the discrete-time scattering formalism, analyzes the non-Hermitian skin effect in the reflection matrix, connects the GH shift to the bulk topological invariant, and verifies the results across trivial, Chern, and anomalous phases.
why_interesting: It provides a novel real-space, boundary-based diagnostic for non-equilibrium topology, which is highly relevant for characterizing driven systems in AMO and quantum optics without needing full momentum-space tomography.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of the multi-step driving lattice model and scattering settings. (b) Quasienergy band structures ϵ(ky) for a strip geometry with open boundary conditions along the x direction and periodic boundary conditions in the y direction. Three representative hopping amplitudes are shown from left to right: J = 0.5π/τ [trivial phase with W3(0) = 0 and W3(π) = 0], J = 1.5π/τ [Floquet Chern insu- lator phase, with W3(0) = 0 and W3(π) = 1], and J = 2.5π/τ [anomalous Floquet phase, with W3(0) = 1 and W3(π) = 1]. The system size along x direction is Lx = 60.

FIG. 2. Eigenspectra of the reflection matrix across differ- ent Floquet topological phases in the complex plane. Blue and red dots represent the reflection spectra obtained un- der transverse periodic (strip geometry) and open boundary conditions, respectively. (a),(c),(e) Reflection spectra evalu- ated for an incident energy ϵ = π for the trivial phase (J = 0.5π/τ), the Floquet Chern insulator phase (J = 1.5π/τ), and the anomalous Floquet phase (J = 2.5π/τ), respectively. (b),(d),(f) Corresponding spectra for the same three phases, but with the incident energy ϵ = 0. The system size is Lx = 60 along x direction.

FIG. 3. Spatial profiles of the reflection eigenvectors un- der open boundary conditions. (a),(c),(e) Distributions of all eigenvectors of the reflection matrix for the trivial phase, the Floquet Chern insulator phase, and the anomalous Floquet phase, respectively, evaluated for an incident energy ϵ = π. (b),(d),(f) Corresponding profiles for the same three phases, but with the incident energy ϵ = 0. The system size is Lx = Ly = 60.

FIG. 4. Goos-H¨anchen shift of the reflection waves. Momentum-dependent GH shift ∆GH(ϵ0, ky0) as a function of the central transverse momentum ky0. (a),(c),(e) GH shifts evaluated for an incident energy ϵ0 = π for the trivial phase (J = 0.5π/τ), the Floquet Chern insulator phase (J = 1.5π/τ), and the anomalous Floquet phase (J = 2.5π/τ), respectively. (b),(d),(f) Corresponding GH shifts for the same three phases, but with the incident energy ϵ0 = 0. The numerical simulations are performed with a system size Lx = Ly = 60 and a wave-packet width σ = 6.
